---
title: "ETC5512_Tutorial4"
date: 1 Apr 2026
bibliography: references.bib
format: 
  html: 
    toc: true
---

# Task 1: Working with Figures

```{r}
#| label: fig-penguins-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Body mass vs. Flipper length for three penguin species."
#| warning: false
library(ggplot2)
library(palmerpenguins)

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()
```

@fig-penguins-scatter illustrates the relationship between body mass and flipper length got different penguin species

# Task 2: Creating Professional Tables

```{r}
#| label: tbl-penguin-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Mean body mass of penguins by species and sex."
library(dplyr)

penguins |>
  filter(!is.na(sex)) |>
  group_by(species, sex) |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g)) |>
  knitr::kable()
```

@tbl-penguin-summary summaries the body mass of penguins by species and sex.

# Task 3: Dynamic Cross-Referencing
As shown in @fig-penguins-scatter, there is a clear positive correlation between flipper length and body mass. This means when the flipper length increases, the penguins are heavier.

# Extension: Academic Referencing
*Parenthetical:* The data was originally collected by Gorman et al. [@penguins_data]

*Narrative:* @penguins_data argues that the increase of flipper length can increase the weight of penguins.

# Note:
We can use the below link to create the template for bib file
Ex we can just copy the link of articles and then paste to the site
<https://zbib.org>

# Add photo 
![Koala](https://www.wwf.org.uk/sites/default/files/styles/social_share_image/public/2022-05/_WW236934.jpg?itok=OD_YT_by)

```{r}
#| label: fig-penguin-photo
#| fig-cap: "Penguin"
knitr::include_graphics("https://s.france24.com/media/display/544355b0-45df-11f0-9098-005056a97e36/w:980/Part-GTY-GYI0061951038-1-1-0.jpg")
```

See @fig-penguin-photo for the penguin image.
